old_claim.note: Remote Patient Monitoring provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: CGMs require a glucose-sensing element; the first FDA-approved CGM systems used sensor technology to measure glucose continuously rather than occasional finger-stick readings.

source_shape.source_locator: FDA Eversense CGM executive summary background on previously approved CGMs and sensor-based continuous glucose monitoring.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The FDA summary states that the first CGM was the Medtronic MiniMed approved in 1999 and describes CGM as continuous monitoring accomplished by glucose-sensing sensor technology.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports replacing RPM with glucose biosensors because the CGM product depends on the glucose sensor component; RPM is a downstream care model that can consume CGM data.

ontology_before: Continuous glucose monitors depended on remote patient monitoring, reversing a product/application relationship.
ontology_after: Continuous glucose monitors depend on glucose biosensors, while remote monitoring remains a broader connected-care workflow.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: CGM no longer directly depends on remote patient monitoring and now requires glucose biosensors.
would_reject_if: The node were re-scoped to a remote monitoring platform that displays CGM readings rather than CGM devices.
A source showed continuous glucose monitoring can exist without any glucose-sensing element.
The graph reintroduced remote patient monitoring as a direct CGM prerequisite.
short_reason: CGMs are sensor devices that can feed RPM; they should not depend on RPM.
